Please select sectors and regions first: Veuillez d’abord sélectionner des secteurs et des régions
Update Map: Mettre à jour la carte du monde
Enter Python code below. Press <b>Enter<: Saisissez le code Python ci-dessous. Appuyez sur la touche Entrée pour l’exécuter et utilisez Maj+Entrée pour insérer une nouvelle ligne dans une instruction sur plusieurs lignes.
Stage Analysis: Analyse par niveaux de la chaîne de valeur
Value chain stages: Niveaux de la chaîne de valeur
Include units in column names: Inclure les unités dans les noms de colonnes
Veuillez sélectionner au moins un impact.
Continuous: Colors and legend use absolu: Continu : couleurs et légende basées sur des valeurs absolues. Le contraste se règle via normalisation + troncature robuste + gamma.
Binned: Discrete classes. With 'Relative: Par classes : classes discrètes. Avec « Relatif », la classification se fait par pourcentages (somme = 100 %) ; sinon, par valeurs absolues.
Choisissez une palette de couleurs pour la carte.
Affiche l’échelle de couleurs avec des plages de valeurs.
Titre optionnel au-dessus de la carte.
Choose between binned (discrete classes): Choisissez entre classes discrètes (par classes) et échelle continue (continu).
If checked, classes use percentage share: Si activé, les classes sont formées selon des parts en pourcentage (somme = 100 %) ; sinon selon des valeurs absolues.
Number of classes when no custom bins ar: Nombre de classes si aucun seuil personnalisé n’est indiqué.
Seuils internes des classes séparés par des virgules. Le minimum et le maximum sont ajoutés automatiquement.
linear: uniform steps | log: highlight o: linéaire : pas réguliers | log : met l’accent sur les ordres de grandeur (logarithmique) | puissance : contraste ajustable avec gamma
Quantile clipping for vmin/vmax (e.g., 2: Détermine vmin/vmax via des quantiles (p. ex. 2 % utilise les 2e et 98e percentiles).
PowerNorm exponent: <1 enhances small va: Exposant pour PowerNorm : <1 met en avant les petites valeurs, >1 met en avant les grandes valeurs.
Colormap used to color the countries. Us: Palette pour colorer les pays. Avec « Inverser », l’ordre clair/foncé est inversé.
Displays a legend. In continuous mode it: Affiche une légende. En mode « continu », elle montre des valeurs absolues ; en mode « par classes », elle montre des plages numériques de classes.
continuous: Colors reflect absolute valu: continu : les couleurs reflètent des valeurs absolues ; la légende est continue. par classes : classes discrètes. Avec « Relatif », le découpage se fait par pourcentages.
Binned mode only: toggle between absolut: Uniquement en mode par classes : bascule entre valeurs absolues et pourcentages pour les seuils de classes.
Number of discrete classes. If 'Custom b: Nombre de classes discrètes. Si « Classes personnalisées » est renseigné, celles-ci priment.
Enter inner edges like '10, 25, 50'. The: Saisissez des seuils internes, p. ex. « 10, 25, 50 ». Le minimum et le maximum sont ajoutés automatiquement.
Normalization for continuous mode:\n- li: Normalisation pour le mode continu : - linéaire : mappage uniforme de vmin..vmax - log : mappage logarithmique ; uniquement pour des valeurs strictement positives - puissance : mappage en puissance ; voir Gamma
Robust clipping determines vmin/vmax fro: La troncature robuste détermine vmin/vmax via des quantiles plutôt que via min/max afin d’atténuer les valeurs aberrantes.
Gamma (PowerNorm):\n- gamma < 1 emphasis: Gamma (PowerNorm) : - gamma < 1 met en avant la partie basse - gamma > 1 met en avant la partie haute
Linear: uniform color steps from vmin to: Linéaire : dégradé de couleurs régulier de vmin à vmax.
Log: emphasises orders of magnitude; req: Log : met l’accent sur les ordres de grandeur ; nécessite des données strictement positives.
Power: adjust contrast with Gamma (<1: h: Puissance : contrôle du contraste via gamma (<1 : met en avant les petites valeurs ; >1 : met en avant les grandes).
topflop.settings.intro: Le premier impact détermine l’ordre. Via le bouton « Comparer avec d’autres impacts », on peut ajouter jusqu’à trois impacts de comparaison.
Use minimum share threshold: Utiliser un seuil de part minimale
pie.settings.intro: Configurez comment les tranches sont sélectionnées, triées et affichées. Astuce : si une part minimale est activée, la limite « Tranches principales » est ignorée.
pie.settings.limit.explain.top: Mode tranches principales : les plus grandes tranches sont affichées, le reste est regroupé sous « Autres ».
pie.settings.limit.explain.threshold: Mode seuil : les parts en dessous de la part minimale sont regroupées sous « Autres ».
Help.Title: Aide & Guide
Help.Subtitle: Quick start: selection, visualisation and contribution analysis
Help.Intro.Title: What is EXIOBASE Explorer?
Help.Intro.Body:
EXIOBASE Explorer helps you understand global supply chains.

You select regions and sectors and then see which environmental and economic indicators (e.g. value added, emissions, working hours) are connected to them.

Important: "Absolute" are real amounts (e.g. euros, kg). "Relative" shows shares in % (sum = 100%).
Help.Selection.Title: 1) Selection
Help.Selection.Body:
In the "Selection" tab you choose countries/regions and economic sectors.

Tip: You can also select nothing — then results are computed globally.
Help.Visualisation.Title: 2) Visualisation
Help.Visualisation.Body:
Here you can open different analyses: by value chain stages or by regions.

Each analysis can have multiple tabs — you can rename or close them.
Help.Map.Title: 3) Maps (World map)
Help.Map.Body:
The world map colors countries by a value.

- "Relative" shows each country’s share of the global total.
- "Per capita" also divides by population (if available).

Units are automatically scaled to readable values (e.g. million €, kg, t).
Help.Contrib.Title: 4) Contribution analysis
Help.Contrib.Body:
Click a country (or bars/slices) to see which sectors/regions contribute most to a value.

This helps you understand why a country has a high value.
Help.Tips.Title: Tips
Help.Tips.Body:
- Start with a few indicators, e.g. value added or greenhouse gas emissions.
- Switch between absolute/relative to better understand magnitudes.
- Use per-capita for fairer comparisons between big and small countries.
StageTab.Intro: Shows how selected indicators are distributed across value chain stages (in %). Click a cell for contribution analysis.
RegionTab.Intro: Analyse regions: map, top/flop or pie chart. Click a country/bar/slice for contribution analysis.
RegionTab.MapIntro: World map settings. Tip: "Relative" is great for comparisons, "Absolute" shows real amounts.
RegionTab.ContinuousHint: Continuous uses absolute values (or per capita, if available).
Name: Name
SettingsTab.Explain: Set year and language here. This affects selection and analyses.
education_mode_description: Ajoute des explications et du contenu pédagogique pour la classe.
education_disabled_description: Active le mode éducation dans les paramètres pour voir le module d’apprentissage.
education_placeholder_basics: This section will introduce supply chains, indirect effects, and the idea of input-output analysis.
education_placeholder_visualizations: This section will explain how to interpret maps, bubble charts, and relative versus absolute values.
education_placeholder_simulation: This section will later contain a small interactive model for experimenting with simplified input-output tables.
education_basics_intro: Modern products rely on complex global supply chains. Input–output analysis helps us trace how sectors and countries depend on each other.
education_card_supply_chain_text: A supply chain is the network of steps and suppliers needed to make a product — from raw materials to final assembly and transport.
education_card_sectors_text: Even a simple product uses many inputs (energy, metals, services). Because countries trade, part of the production happens abroad.
education_card_io_table_text: An input–output table summarizes who buys from whom in an economy: how much each sector purchases from every other sector as inputs.
education_card_direct_indirect_text: Direct effects happen in the sector you select. Indirect effects are the upstream requirements in all supplying sectors needed to deliver one unit of output.
education_card_global_impacts_text: Emissions and resource use can occur far away from the final consumer, because production steps happen where inputs are produced.
education_basics_bridge_text: In the Explorer, the same logic is applied to real EXIOBASE data to quantify impacts across regions, sectors, and value-chain stages.
education_visualizations_intro: The Explorer’s charts show where impacts arise along the supply chain. These notes help you interpret maps and diagrams correctly.
education_card_maps_text: Colors represent the magnitude of a value for each country. Darker colors indicate larger values. The value corresponds to impacts along the supply chain linked to your selection.
education_card_bubbles_text: Each column represents a stage (e.g. resource extraction, preliminary products, suppliers, retail/final demand). Bubble size indicates relative share; percentage values describe how the total is distributed across stages.
education_card_pie_text: Each slice represents a region’s contribution to the total. "Others" groups many small contributions that are too small to show individually.
education_card_absolute_relative_text: Absolute values are totals (e.g. kg CO₂, euros). Relative values are percentage shares (sum = 100%). A high percentage does not necessarily mean a large absolute impact if the overall total is small.
education_simulation_intro:
This simplified toy model shows how final demand triggers production across all sectors – including indirect supply-chain effects.

Important: This is a classroom model (3 sectors), not an EXIOBASE calculation.
education_simulation_sectors_hint: Each cell describes how much input from the row sector is required to produce one unit in the column sector.
education_simulation_invalid_model_text: These coefficients make the model invalid or numerically unstable. Reduce some values and try again.
education_simulation_invalid_number_text: Some inputs are not valid numbers. They are temporarily treated as 0 until you correct them.
education_simulation_details_text: We compute total production x from x = (I – A)^(-1) · y. Indirect effects are x − y. Per-sector impact is f × x (and summed across sectors).
education_simulation_learning_note: Learning note: This mini model is intentionally simplified. Even small changes can influence the overall result.
education_course_title: Learning input-output logic with the EXIOBASE Explorer
education_course_intro: This tab is structured like a short learning module. It guides you through global supply chains, input-output logic, the Leontief idea, environmental extensions, and MRIO – and shows how the Explorer builds its analyses on these concepts.
education_course_index_hint: Chapters build on each other: from the guiding problem to the model and its application in the Explorer.
education_course_footer_note: Note: The visuals and the mini model are intentionally simplified for teaching. They explain the idea – not every detail of the EXIOBASE database.
education_ch1_intro: Start with the core idea: consumption sets supply chains in motion – and therefore environmental impacts.
education_ch1_block_exiobase_text:
EXIOBASE is a large database describing the global economy. It captures how industries in different countries are connected.

It contains (1) economic transactions between industries (who delivers what to whom) and (2) environmental and social indicators (e.g. emissions, water, land, labor).

Technical term: EXIOBASE is a Multi-Regional Input-Output (MRIO) database.
education_ch1_block_explorer_text:
The Explorer is an interactive tool to explore these data. You choose regions, industries, and indicators – and the Explorer computes the supply-chain effects.

This lets you see where production happens and where environmental impacts occur – not only directly, but also indirectly in supplier industries.

Why this matters: it helps explain how consumption decisions can cause impacts across global supply chains.
education_ch2_intro: What is a supply chain – and why is it so hard to see where the impacts of our consumption actually occur?
education_ch2_block_flow_text: In the input-output view, sectors represent production activities of an economy. A sector uses inputs (goods and services) and produces outputs that other sectors or final users consume.
education_ch2_block_sectors_text: What matters is not only what a sector does directly, but also what it purchases. When final demand increases, production and impacts often rise in supplier sectors as well – these are indirect effects.
education_ch3_intro: Input–output tables are an accounting view of the economy: who supplies whom – and how large is final demand?
education_ch3_block_table_title: How to read an IO table
education_ch3_block_table_text:
To describe these supply relationships for many sectors at once, we need a systematic representation.

That is what the IO table provides: it combines inter-industry transactions, final demand, and primary inputs/value added in one place.
education_ch3_block_interpret_text: The table is the basis for a calculation: from final demand we can derive total output for each sector when all intermediate requirements are included.
education_ch4_intro: The core idea is simple: final demand starts the chain reaction, and the model propagates it through dependent sectors.
education_ch4_block_equations_title: From final demand to total output
education_ch4_block_equations_text: In words: total output (x) covers intermediate inputs (A·x) plus final demand (y). If y increases, x must increase – even in sectors that are not demanded directly. The inverse matrix (I − A)^(-1) summarizes this cascade of requirements.
education_ch5_intro: Economic production can be linked to environmental indicators – allowing us to estimate impacts of consumption and investment.
education_ch5_block_extensions_text: A simple idea: each sector has a direct intensity (f), for example kg CO₂ per euro of output. Combining f with computed total output x yields an estimated total impact. Indirect effects are included automatically through x.
education_ch6_intro: EXIOBASE is multi-regional: sectors exist in many countries and trade connects them. Therefore final demand in one country can trigger production and impacts elsewhere.
education_ch6_block_mrio_text: Instead of one economy, MRIO represents many regions at once. Trade links these regions through supply relationships. This explains why impacts of consumption can occur abroad.
education_ch6_block_why_maps_text: Because the data are region-resolved, the Explorer can show where impacts occur – for example as a world map or top/bottom lists.
education_ch6_block_analyses_text: Region analysis asks: in which countries/regions do impacts occur? Stage analysis asks: at which supply-chain stage do they occur (e.g. raw materials, preliminaries, suppliers, trade)?
education_ch7_intro: Try the idea yourself: change final demand and intensities and immediately see how production, indirect effects, and impacts shift.
education_ch7_block_sim_text: The mini model is intentionally small so the logic is visible. Final demand y is the main driver. You can inspect and edit the technical sector dependencies (A) in the optional model details.
education_ch7_sim_focus_note: Didactic focus: imagine households, government, or firms demand more from one sector. Then not only that sector produces more – suppliers must produce more as well.
education_ch7_demand_hint: Tip: increase final demand in one sector and observe how other sectors respond via intermediate inputs.
education_ch7_intensity_hint: Tip: change intensities to simulate cleaner or dirtier production.
education_ch7_model_details_intro: The A matrix describes intermediate requirements per unit of output. If values become too large, the model can become mathematically invalid (then it will not be computed).
education_diagram_supplychain_caption: In the model we focus on sectors (not individual firms). Inputs can come from many sectors and regions.
education_diagram_dependency_caption: Sectors are connected: agriculture buys machinery and services; industry needs raw materials and energy; services use industrial products.
education_diagram_table_legend_intermedi: Flows between producing sectors (inputs for production).
education_diagram_table_legend_final_tex: Demand outside intermediate production (e.g. households, investment, exports).
education_diagram_equations_caption: Leontief logic links final demand and total output. Indirect requirements are captured by the term (I − A)^(-1).
education_diagram_extension_caption: Environmental extension: with intensities (f), total output (x) can be turned into total impact (f × x).
education_diagram_mrio_caption: MRIO links regions: final demand in one region can trigger production and environmental impacts in other regions.
education_diagram_region_analysis_text: Shows where impacts occur geographically (e.g. maps, top/bottom, pie charts).
education_diagram_stage_analysis_text: Shows at which supply-chain stage impacts occur (e.g. raw materials, preliminaries, suppliers, trade).
education_ch3_block_orientation_title: 1) How an IO table is organized
education_ch3_block_orientation_text:
Think of the table as a map of deliveries. Rows represent deliveries (who supplies?), columns represent inputs (who buys?).

A full IO table also contains a separate block for primary inputs/value added (e.g. labor, taxes). These do not come from producing sectors in the same way intermediate inputs do.
education_ch3_block_intermediate_text:
The large block in the top-left shows deliveries between sectors. When sector j produces, it purchases inputs from many sectors i.

We denote these transactions by z_ij. They are the basis of interdependence in the model.
education_ch3_eq_intermediate_definition: z_ij: delivery from sector i to sector j (intermediate input)
education_ch3_block_final_text:
Final demand is demand outside intermediate production: for example household consumption, government demand, investment, and exports.

In an IO table these are separate columns to the right of the inter-industry block. Together they form a final-demand value y_i for each sector i.
education_ch3_eq_final_demand_definition: y_i: demand outside intermediate production (consumption, government, investment, exports)
education_ch3_block_primary_inputs_text:
Besides intermediate inputs, production uses primary inputs that are not outputs of producing sectors (e.g. labor, taxes, capital income as part of value added).

IO tables usually list these as rows below the sector rows. Together with intermediate inputs, they describe the cost structure of production.
education_ch3_block_accounting_title: 5) Accounting logic: where does output go?
education_ch3_block_accounting_text:
Before we use formulas, a simple idea is enough: every unit of output must go somewhere.

Some of a sector’s output goes to other sectors as intermediate inputs. The rest goes to final demand.
education_ch3_block_more_than_table_titl: 6) Why this is more than a table
education_ch3_block_more_than_table_text: If we know how much input is required per unit of output, we can derive how strongly all sectors must produce to satisfy final demand – including indirect supply-chain effects.
education_ch3_block_coefficients_text:
Next, we turn money flows into a production „recipe”.

A technical coefficient a_ij answers: how much input from i does sector j need per unit of output? This lets us scale production up or down without re-reading the full table each time.
education_ch3_block_assumption_text:
Now comes the model assumption: input proportions stay (roughly) constant.

If a sector doubles output, it needs (roughly) double the intermediate inputs at the same technology.
education_ch3_block_to_leontief_title: 9) From A to the Leontief equation (and why y matters)
education_ch3_block_to_leontief_text:
Now we connect everything step by step.

1) Start: total output equals deliveries to industries plus final demand.
2) Replace z_ij with a_ij · x_j.
3) In matrix form this becomes x = A·x + y.
4) Rearranging yields x = (I − A)^(-1)·y.
education_ch3_block_bridge_text: The simulation later is not meant to feel like raw table editing. It focuses on y: you change final demand, and the model immediately shows how total output (x), indirect effects, and later environmental impacts change.
education_diagram_table_orientation_capt: Columns show a sector’s inputs. Rows show a sector’s deliveries. Primary inputs (e.g. labor) form a distinct block below the sectors.
education_diagram_table_legend_primary_t: Inputs that are not outputs of producing sectors (e.g. labor, taxes, value added).
education_course_guiding_question_text: How can consumption in one region trigger production and environmental impacts across many sectors and regions – and how does EXIOBASE make these connections measurable and visible?
education_course_card_ch1: What this is about: EXIOBASE, the Explorer, and the idea of global interdependence.
education_course_card_ch2: Supply chains as networks: inputs → production → outputs – across sectors and countries.
education_course_card_ch3: Reading an IO table: intermediate demand, final demand, value added – and the step to the A matrix.
education_course_card_ch4: Final demand as the driver: from y to total output x via Leontief logic.
education_course_card_ch5: Linking output to indicators: intensities connect production to environmental impacts.
education_course_card_ch6: Why global: MRIO links regions – enabling maps, region analysis, and stage analysis.
education_course_card_ch7: Try it yourself: change y, see system-wide effects – apply the concepts from the chapters.
education_course_overview_hint: You can follow the chapters in order – or jump directly to the mini model in Chapter 7.
education_ch2_block_example_title: A smartphone as a supply-chain example
education_ch2_block_example_text:
A supply chain is the sequence (and often a network) of steps needed to make a product – from raw materials to components, assembly, and sale.

Importantly, these steps often happen in different countries. A smartphone is a useful example.
education_ch2_block_problems_title: Why this matters: complexity, opacity, and distance
education_ch2_block_problems_text:
Globalization has made supply chains longer and more complex. That makes it harder to trace where production happens and what environmental impacts occur.

Consumption and impacts are often spatially separated: we buy here, but parts of the burden happen elsewhere.
education_diagram_phone_map_subtitle: Example: steps occur in different world regions
education_diagram_phone_caption: This example is simplified: in reality there are many suppliers and routes. The key idea remains: one product connects production steps across the world.
education_diagram_problem_intransparency: Many steps and suppliers make it hard to know where a product is made – and what impacts occur along the way.
education_diagram_problem_decoupling_tex: Consumption and production are often in different regions. As a result, impacts frequently occur far away from where the product is purchased.
education_diagram_problem_caption: This is where input-output analysis helps: it quantifies these indirect and globally distributed impacts.
education_diagram_phone_map_hint_text: Each marker represents a typical step in the supply chain. Regions are examples – a real supply chain can be much more complex.
education_ch1_visual_caption: The chapters explain step by step how data and models can trace this chain.
education_ch1_exiobase_eu_funding_note: EXIOBASE was developed with support from EU research funding.
education_ch3_callout_demand_types: There are two types of demand: intermediate demand (between industries) and final demand (outside intermediate production).
education_ch3_callout_accounting: For each sector: total output = deliveries to industries + deliveries to final demand.
education_ch3_callout_coefficients: Technical coefficients describe the input structure of production – like a recipe.
education_ch3_takeaway_title: Why final demand (y) is the key driver
education_ch3_takeaway_text:
Once the economic structure (A) is known, we only need y.

Then we can compute how much total production is required (x) – and therefore how strongly the supply chain responds.
education_ch3_callout_final_demand: Once A is known, x can be computed from y. y is the driver.
education_ch3_diagram_final_demand_capti: Idea: final demand triggers production – and indirect effects arise through the supply chain.
education_ch3_diagram_coeff_caption: Technical coefficients mean: inputs scale with output (at the same technology).
education_ch3_intro_sectors_text:
To simplify the global economy, we group similar types of production into sectors.

A sector is not a single firm. It is a category (e.g. agriculture, industry, services).
education_ch3_sector_examples_caption: Sectors help describe complex supply chains as a manageable system.
education_ch3_intro_io_flow_text:
Production is a transformation process: a sector needs inputs (goods and services) to produce outputs.

Many inputs come from other sectors – that is exactly what creates supply chains.
education_ch3_diagram_io_flow_caption: Input-output models track these flows systematically – not only inside one firm, but across the whole economy.
education_ch3_intro_interdependence_text:
Sectors are connected because they purchase from each other.

Example: agriculture uses machinery (industry) and logistics (services). Industry uses raw inputs (agriculture) and services. Services use industrial products.
education_ch3_diagram_interdependence_ca: Supply chains exist because inputs come from many sectors. This interdependence is the basis of IO analysis.
